textPlain: > It’s more than just tech. The Saturday increase shows up across sectors among SF-based companies on Ramp, not only softwareHeadline: SF tech workers are working Saturdays
The y axis only goes up to 0.4%
I'll take propaganda trying to normalize the idea of 996 for 200, Alex.
> All data is available for download from Ramp Economics Lab.It's not, though?

At this point, it's obvious and clear that you can outwork these 996 companies by simply requiring employees work 7 days a week in the office.What is preventing one of these 996 companies from doing that and taking the lead in their respective AI niche? If they really believe that an additional day is their competitive edge, that seems like a really easy moat to overcome for a competitor, and by that logic why stop there? Wouldn't you want to maximize your chances of success by requiring your employees work 7 days in the office?Just have your employees work a full 7 days in the office. I'm not joking either. Would some CEO who has adopted this practice care to explain why they don't just make things simple and require their employees to report to the office 7 days per week?
when your credit card is telling people where and when you work it is time to reconsider your credit card
Sure, let's normalize this, think of the shareholders...
20% more effective employees thanks to AI

This article is attempting to make some kind of statement about 996 and "look, now it's here!"But this is plainly ridiculous. The Bay Area has been full of high achievers the entire time I've lived here (since the 20th century). All the startups I worked at, people would work Saturdays. Not all the time, of course, but it was quite common.
This is the type of data analysis I would not want to have done on employees and companies data.
[dead]
[flagged]
Ten years ago, I used to work Saturdays, but mostly out of enjoyment. This is a fun profession!
The new trend is called 007.
I don't think there's a credit card company that can't (or won't) do this. Or even debit cards for the matter. Or any app that has location enabled.
Must be the AI that replaced all the workers that’s ordering all that food

The article talks about how they are controlling for the variation across time, and they’re reporting a new signal. So even if everyone was working Saturdays before, everyone is more working Saturdays now. (Edited typos.)
I, too, often write of cities I’ve clearly never visited.
> public areas are full of fecesI haven't seen any feces in public in SF for at couple of years now.  Yes, it was a problem, but it doesn't seem to be anymore.

I did a huge amount of travel at various times and never had qualms about checking email or doing some other tasks optionally on days off from time to time, but never considered myself to be "working" on weekends.
